reasoning: The vulnerability is reachable and exploitable at default configuration with no non-default options required. The module's default export at line 131 is a public API function directly callable by any consumer. The symlink branch at lines 120-121 (`fsP.symlink(x.linkname, dest)`) is reached whenever an archive entry has `type === 'symlink'` — this requires no special options; the default `opts.plugins` array includes all decompressors (tar, tarbz2, targz, unzip). The guards at lines 89-110 use `safeMakeDir` and `realpath`-based containment checks exclusively on `dest` (the symlink's file-system location within the output directory) — none of these checks touch `x.linkname` (the symlink's target/pointer value). `preventWritingThroughSymlink` only prevents writing *through* a pre-existing symlink at `dest`; it does not validate the new symlink's target. There is no `path.isAbsolute`, `path.resolve`, `startsWith`, schema validation (joi/zod/ajv), or any other guard applied to `x.linkname` anywhere in the call chain. An attacker who can supply the archive (e.g., via file upload, URL fetch, or any input channel) controls `x.linkname` entirely and can set it to an absolute path such as `/etc/passwd` or `/etc/shadow`. The symlink will be created at `dest` (inside the output directory, passing all checks) while pointing externally. Any subsequent dereference by the consuming application reads the attacker-chosen file. No authentication, no privilege, and no non-default option is required to trigger this path.
